common_name: Sun Gold Tomato (Sungold Cherry Tomato, Orange Cherry Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile seed mix; transplant after all frost danger passes in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sun Gold is consistently rated among the sweetest, most intensely flavored cherry tomatoes in existence — an F1 hybrid producing small, tangerine-orange, bite-sized fruits with extraordinarily high sugar content and a complex, tropical-sweet, apricot-citrus flavor.

directions: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun with strong support; harvest clusters of cherry tomatoes when bright tangerine-orange. Solanum lycopersicum 'Sun Gold' (Sun Gold cherry tomato) is arguably the most beloved cherry tomato in home gardening — a Japanese F1 hybrid that consistently receives top ratings for flavor in nearly every comparative tasting. The fruits are small (3/4 to 1 inch), round to slightly oblong, brilliant tangerine-orange when ripe, and remarkably sweet — consistently the highest sugar content among widely grown cherry tomato varieties — with a complex, aromatic flavor combining intense sweetness with tropical-fruit and apricot notes and just enough acid to balance. The flavor is so intense that eating a ripe Sun Gold warm from the vine is often cited by gardeners as a transformative experience — the difference from a grocery store tomato is dramatic. 'Sun Gold' is an indeterminate vine that grows vigorously (staking required), produces prolifically throughout the season, and sets fruit reliably in a wide range of conditions. The thin skin causes occasional cracking near harvest in wet conditions — harvest promptly when ripe. In Gulf Coast zone 9, grow in spring (March–June harvest) and again in fall (September–November harvest), skipping the hottest summer months when fruiting stalls. A must-grow for anyone who wants to understand what a truly great tomato tastes like.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Variety Trials

planting.note: Plant seedlings in spring in deep, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; Palmyra palm is one of the most economically important and versatile palms in all of Asia and Africa — documented to yield more than 800 distinct products from its sap, fruit, leaves, and timber — it is the national tree of Cambodia (Kingdom of Cambodge) and the state tree of Tamil Nadu, India.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; Palmyra palm is remarkably drought-tolerant once established. Borassus flabellifer (Palmyra palm or toddy palm) is one of the oldest cultivated plants in Asia — documented in Tamil literature for over 2,000 years — and one of the most economically important multi-use palms in the tropics. The tree is the national tree of Cambodia and the state tree of Tamil Nadu (India), reflecting its profound cultural and economic significance. The sap (toddy) tapped from the immature flower stalks is an extremely important product: fresh, it is a sweet, mildly alcoholic fermented drink; boiled down, it produces palm jaggery (tan-neera sugar), one of the most traditional sweeteners of South and Southeast Asia; left to ferment fully, it produces palm wine. The young, firm, jelly-textured fruit segments inside the unripe fruit are 'ice apples' (nungu) — refreshing, mildly sweet, hydrating; eaten as a summer cooling food throughout South India. The ripe orange-yellow outer flesh is edible; seeds are cooked and eaten when young. The leaves are used for traditional palm-leaf manuscripts (the medium of Southeast Asian scripture and literature for millennia), baskets, mats, and roof thatching. A truly remarkable plant of civilization.

common_name: Chilean Wine Palm (Coquito Palm, Honey Palm)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Chilean wine palm is the world's most cold-hardy large palm, tolerating temperatures down to approximately 20°F (-7°C, zone 8 briefly) — its exceptionally massive trunk stores huge quantities of water and nutrients that protect it from cold; it is extremely slow-growing and may take 40–80 years to reach full height.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; exceedingly drought-tolerant once established. Jubaea chilensis (Chilean wine palm or coquito palm) is the world's most cold-hardy large-growing palm species — native to the central Chilean Andes coastal valleys where it experiences occasional frost and cold winters — and the most massive palm in terms of trunk girth, with a single trunk reaching 3–5 feet in diameter at maturity. It tolerates temperatures briefly to approximately 18–20°F (zone 8) — making it growable outdoors in zone 8 or warmer, far colder than any other large pinnate palm. The 'wine' in the common name refers to the traditional Chilean practice of felling mature trees to tap the sap as it flows from the cut stump — producing large quantities of sweet sap boiled to 'miel de palma' (palm honey, a thick, dark, amber, molasses-like sweetener), or fermented to palm wine ('chicha de palma'). CAUTION: The traditional sap harvest requires killing the tree. The small fruits ('coquitos,' little coconuts) contain a sweet, coconut-flavored seed that is sold commercially as a snack in Chilean markets. Jubaea is critically endangered in the wild due to centuries of sap-tapping and habitat loss; it is protected in Chilean national reserves. An extraordinary garden palm for warm-temperate climates where most large palms fail.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Palms in California

common_name: Toddy Fishtail Palm (Wine Palm, Jaggery Palm)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; toddy fishtail palm is an Indian and Sri Lankan multi-use palm grown for palm wine (toddy), jaggery sugar production, and as an ornamental with distinctive bipinnate (doubly compound) leaves with unique fish-tail-shaped leaflets — it is monocarpic, dying after it completes a single multi-year flowering cycle.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; note that Caryota urens is monocarpic and dies after completing its single flowering sequence. Caryota urens (toddy fishtail palm or wine palm) is a large, single-stemmed Indian and Sri Lankan palm remarkable for several unique characteristics: it is one of very few palms with bipinnate leaves (doubly compound — each leaf divided into segments, each segment then further divided into individual fishtail-shaped leaflets), giving it a dramatically different appearance from most other palms; and it is monocarpic — it flowers only once in its life, then dies. The flowering sequence begins at the top of the trunk and proceeds downward over several years (from the highest leaf axil to the lowest), with the entire tree dying when the last inflorescence at the base completes seed set — a spectacular but terminal process. The palm is the primary source of kithul jaggery in Sri Lanka (considered the finest quality palm sugar in Asia) and a major source of toddy (palm wine) in South India. The sap from tapped flower stalks is collected daily; fresh it is sweet and mildly fermenting; processed it produces kithul honey, jaggery, and vinegar. The seeds and unripe fruits contain oxalate crystals — do not eat raw. The palm is ornamentally outstanding — unique bipinnate fronds create an unmistakable tropical texture.
sources: Sri Lanka Department of Agriculture — Kithul Palm
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Caryota urens

common_name: Rose of Venezuela (Scarlet Flame Bean, Mountain Rose)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade to full sun; Rose of Venezuela is a South American leguminous tree considered one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the tropics — it produces large, tightly-packed, dome-shaped clusters of brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers directly on the branches and trunk, and displays new leaves as hanging, pale-pink 'ribbons' or 'tassels' before they harden to green.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade to full sun; provide irrigation during dry periods. Brownea coccinea (Rose of Venezuela or scarlet flame bean) is a tropical South American tree widely regarded as one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the tropics — a standard in tropical botanical gardens worldwide precisely because of two extraordinary visual displays. The first: large, dome-shaped, tightly packed heads (capitula) of brilliant scarlet-red, tubular flowers — 6–10 inches across — that emerge directly from the branches and trunk (cauliflory), creating a dense, ball-like cluster of brilliant red against the green foliage. The second: the remarkable display of new leaf growth — young leaves emerge in hanging 'pendant' clusters, beginning as soft, limp, pale-pink to cream-colored 'ribbons' of young leaflets that hang limply below the branch tips before gradually stiffening, darkening to green, and taking their mature position. This juvenile 'drooping' display of new leaves is one of the most unusual and memorable sights in tropical horticulture. Brownea is in the legume family (Fabaceae) and is related to the flamboyant tree. In zone 10–12, it makes an outstanding specimen tree for tropical gardens, botanical collections, and large spaces where a genuinely rare and spectacular ornamental is desired.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Plants of the World Online
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Unusual Tropical Trees

common_name: Hardy Water Canna (Powdery Thalia, White Powdered Thalia)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in water 6–18 inches deep or in consistently saturated, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Hardy water canna is a native North American aquatic perennial of the cypress swamps, pond margins, and wet prairies from Missouri south to Texas — it is the hardiest aquatic member of the canna family and provides outstanding ornamental value and excellent wildlife habitat in water gardens and naturalistic pond plantings.

directions: Plant rhizomes in water up to 18 inches deep or in saturated soil in full sun; divide every 2–3 years to manage spread. Thalia dealbata (hardy water canna or powdery thalia) is a native North American aquatic perennial from the cypress swamps, marshes, shallow ponds, and wet prairies of the south-central United States — native from Missouri and Kansas south through Oklahoma and Texas to the Gulf Coast. The plant produces large (12–18 inch), upright, blue-green, canna-like leaves covered with a distinctive waxy, powdery-white coating (the 'dealbata' = whitened) that gives the foliage a handsome, glaucous blue-green appearance. Slender stalks rise 4–6 feet above the water, bearing small but attractive violet-purple flowers in loose panicles; the seeds that follow are eaten by waterfowl. Thalia dealbata is the hardiest aquatic plant in the canna family (Marantaceae) — surviving winters to zone 5 with rhizomes protected under water. In water gardens, it functions as a beautiful, bold-textured marginal plant providing vertical accent, wildlife habitat (hummingbirds and bees visit the flowers), and dramatic blue-green foliage contrast. In zone 9, it grows in full sun in shallow water or wet soil and spreads gradually from rhizomes to form an attractive colony. A wonderful native aquatic for rain garden oases, retention ponds, and naturalistic water features.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Water Garden Plants

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; Katuk is a Southeast Asian tropical shrub producing exceptionally nutritious, mild-flavored leaves used as a vegetable throughout Malaysia, Vietnam, and Indonesia — the leaves contain up to 7% protein by fresh weight, high levels of vitamins A and C, and a mild, slightly sweet, pea-like flavor that makes them one of the most palatable and nutritious tropical leaf vegetables.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; harvest tender shoot tips and young leaves regularly for continuous production. Sauropus androgynus (katuk or sweet leaf) is a Southeast Asian tropical shrub — native from India through mainland Southeast Asia to Indonesia — that produces one of the most nutritious and palatable leafy vegetables in the tropical world. The shoots and young leaves (the standard harvested part) contain exceptionally high levels of protein (6–8% by fresh weight — remarkably high for a vegetable), vitamins A and C, iron, and calcium; the fresh leaves taste mild, slightly sweet, and pea-like — quite different from the stronger, bitter flavors of many tropical leafy greens. In Malaysia (where it is called 'cekur manis'), Vietnam ('rau ngot'), and Indonesia, katuk is a standard, everyday vegetable used in stir-fries, soups, and as a cooked green. It is also extensively used in Singapore and among Southeast Asian diaspora communities worldwide. CAUTION: Very large quantities of raw juice (as consumed in some weight-loss protocols in Taiwan in the 1990s) caused severe pulmonary toxicity; katuk must be cooked before eating and should not be consumed raw in large amounts or as a supplement. Properly cooked as a vegetable in normal food quantities, it is safe and highly beneficial.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Leafy Greens
Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (MARDI)

planting.note: Direct sow 2–6 weeks after last frost in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; pearl millet is the world's most drought-tolerant cereal grain — it thrives where maize, sorghum, and other grains fail — and is an outstanding summer cover crop for hot, dry, sandy soil in zone 7–12 where it grows to 6–8 feet, fixes atmospheric carbon, suppresses weeds, and improves soil structure.

directions: Direct sow at 10–20 lbs per acre in warm, well-drained, lean soil in full sun; mow or incorporate when 50–60% heading for maximum biomass. Cenchrus americanus (pearl millet or bajra, formerly Pennisetum glaucum) is the world's most drought-tolerant cereal grain crop — an ancient African cultigen domesticated in the Sahel region over 3,000 years ago and now the primary staple grain for hundreds of millions of people in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Pearl millet thrives under extremely high temperatures (up to 110°F), very low and erratic rainfall (8–20 inches annually), and highly infertile, sandy soils — growing successfully where maize, sorghum, and virtually any other grain crop cannot produce. The grain (bajra in India, the primary use) is ground into flour for flatbreads (bajra roti) and porridges; the nutritional profile is excellent (higher in protein, iron, and zinc than maize or sorghum). As a cover crop in zone 7–12, pearl millet is outstanding for hot, dry, sandy, infertile soils where most summer cover crops fail — it grows to 6–8 feet rapidly, produces enormous biomass to incorporate as organic matter, suppresses weeds completely, and requires essentially no inputs. It is also an important wildlife food and game food-plot crop for white-tailed deer, quail, and dove.
sources: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics (ICRISAT)
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pearl Millet
